programs.name: Alabama Medicaid Elderly and Disabled Waiver
programs.what_it_does: Provides home and community-based services to prevent institutionalization for eligible seniors and disabled individuals. Services include personal care, homemaker assistance, respite, adult day health, meal delivery, and companion care through person-centered care plans.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Seniors aged 65+ or disabled individuals requiring nursing facility level of care, Medicaid eligible, and living in the community.[1]
programs.benefit_value: Varies by individualized care plan; covers services equivalent to nursing home care in home setting.[1]

programs.name: Alabama PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.what_it_does: Delivers comprehensive medical and social services to frail seniors in community settings, including adult day health, transportation, and home care. It coordinates all Medicare and Medicaid covered services plus additional support to avoid nursing home admission.
programs.who_its_for: Nursing facility eligible seniors aged 55+ who can live safely in the community with services.
programs.benefit_value: Full capitation coverage of primary, acute, and long-term care services.

programs.name: Alabama Medicaid Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)
programs.what_it_does: Pays Medicare premiums, deductibles, and copayments for eligible low-income seniors. QMB covers Part A/B premiums and cost-sharing; SLMB and QI cover Part B premiums.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income Medicare beneficiaries meeting specific income/asset limits (e.g., 100-135% FPL depending on program).
programs.benefit_value: Premium payments and cost-sharing relief; average annual value $2,000-$5,000 per enrollee.

programs.name: Alabama Elderly Simplified Application Project (AESAP)
programs.what_it_does: Simplifies SNAP/food assistance application for elderly households with no earned income, addressing barriers like mobility and complexity. Improves access to food benefits via mail, fax, email, or online.[3][7]
programs.who_its_for: Households where all members are 60+ with no earned income, at or below federal poverty level.[1][3]
programs.benefit_value: Monthly SNAP benefits scaled to income; average $150-250/month per household.[1]

programs.name: Alabama LIHEAP (Low-Income Home Energy Assistance Program)
programs.what_it_does: Provides financial assistance for heating and cooling bills to low-income households. Administered through local agencies for crisis and regular assistance.
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors 60+ meeting income guidelines (typically 150% FPL).
programs.benefit_value: One-time grants averaging $300-500 per household annually.

programs.name: Alabama Weatherization Assistance Program
programs.what_it_does: Improves energy efficiency in low-income homes through insulation, sealing, and furnace repairs. Reduces utility costs and health risks from poor conditions.[7]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income households including seniors 60+, priority for disabled/vulnerable.
programs.benefit_value: Free home improvements valued at $4,000-7,000 per household.

programs.name: Alabama SHIP (State Health Insurance Assistance Program)
programs.what_it_does: Offers free counseling on Medicare options, plans, and appeals. Helps seniors choose coverage and resolve issues.
programs.who_its_for: All Medicare beneficiaries, especially 65+ low-income seniors.
programs.benefit_value: Free one-on-one counseling; saves average $500+ annually on premiums.

programs.name: Elderly Nutrition Program (Meals on Wheels)
programs.what_it_does: Delivers nutritious meals to homebound seniors and provides congregate meals at senior centers. Operated through 300+ statewide senior centers.[1][8]
programs.who_its_for: Seniors 60+ or disabled, homebound or attending centers, often no/low charge.[1]
programs.benefit_value: 1-2 meals/day; serves 10,844+ seniors annually via Meals on Wheels.[1][8]

programs.name: Alabama Lifespan Respite Program
programs.what_it_does: Provides respite care and support services to family caregivers of seniors/disabled. Builds sustainable respite systems through partnerships and grants.[3][4]
programs.who_its_for: Family caregivers of elderly or disabled adults, coordinated via ADSS and ADRCs.[4]
programs.benefit_value: Varies; temporary relief services, training; federal grant-funded.[4]

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)
programs.what_it_does: Offers part-time community service jobs with training for older low-income workers. Helps transition to unsubsidized employment.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Unemployed low-income individuals 55+, limited work experience.
programs.benefit_value: Minimum wage work experience up to 20 hrs/week.

programs.name: Alabama Legal Services for Seniors
programs.what_it_does: Provides free legal aid for issues like benefits, housing, consumer problems. Accessed via Area Agencies on Aging.
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors 60+ facing legal barriers to services.
programs.benefit_value: Free representation/counseling; resolves benefit denials, exploitation.

programs.name: Alabama Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program
programs.what_it_does: Advocates for residents in nursing homes, assisted living; investigates complaints. Protects from abuse/neglect via statewide network.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Residents of long-term care facilities, families.
programs.benefit_value: Free advocacy; resolves thousands of complaints annually.[1]

programs.name: Alabama Cares Program
programs.what_it_does: Alabama Cares provides support services to unpaid caregivers, including respite care, training, and resources to manage caregiving duties. It helps caregivers of frail adults, those with dementia, or older relative caregivers of children.[2][4]
programs.who_its_for: Unpaid primary caregivers for frail adults age 60+ needing help with 3+ ADLs, individuals with Alzheimer’s/dementia regardless of age, or relative caregivers age 55+ caring for related children.[4]
programs.whats_unique: State-funded caregiver support program administered through Area Agencies on Aging, extending to older relative caregivers of children, beyond standard federal Family Caregiver Support.[2][4][5]
programs.benefit_value: Respite care, counseling, and training services; limited funds available, value based on individual needs.

programs.name: Elderly and Disabled (E&D) Medicaid Waiver
programs.what_it_does: The E&D Waiver provides home and community-based services like personal care, homemaker services, respite care, adult day health, companion services, and home-delivered meals to help seniors avoid nursing home placement. It targets Medicaid recipients who qualify for institutional care.[1][3][5]
programs.who_its_for: Medicaid recipients who are elderly or disabled and meet nursing home level of care criteria.[1][5]
programs.whats_unique: Alabama's state-administered Medicaid waiver program offering tailored long-term home care services, distinct from federal PACE or standard waivers in its specific service mix and state funding integration.[1][3][5]
programs.benefit_value: Services assessed by individual needs; covers personal care, meals, and respite without fixed dollar amount.

programs.name: Senior Aides Program
programs.what_it_does: The Senior Aides program offers part-time employment and training opportunities to eligible low-income older persons. It supports community-based services for seniors through trained aides.[3]
programs.who_its_for: Older persons with limited income, age 60+.[3]
programs.whats_unique: State-specific employment and training initiative for low-income seniors to provide aide services, not replicated as a named program in every state.[3]
programs.benefit_value: Part-time wages and training; specific amounts not detailed.

programs.name: SenioRx Prescription Drug Assistance Program
programs.what_it_does: SenioRx assists Alabama seniors with prescription drug costs through payment support. It helps access medications beyond federal programs.[3]
programs.who_its_for: Alabama seniors, typically low-income or meeting specific criteria.[3]
programs.whats_unique: Alabama's state-funded pharmaceutical assistance program, separate from federal Medicare Part D or low-income subsidies.[3]
programs.benefit_value: Prescription cost assistance; amounts vary by eligibility and drug costs.
